claim: Prostate tumors from patients homozygous for the RNASEL R462Q variant were much more likely to contain xenotropic murine leukemia virus-related virus than prostate tumors from patients with heterozygous or wild-type RNASEL genotypes.
support_summary: Several supportive items trace back to early reports that XMRV was enriched in prostate cancers from men homozygous for RNASEL R462Q. The clearest abstract-level support is from a serologic/PCR/FISH study reporting XMRV-related findings in 8/20 QQ patients versus 3/20 RQ or RR patients, with concordance between serology and prostate-based assays (PMID: 20371060). A prevalence study in sporadic prostate cancer also explicitly cites prior familial prostate cancer data showing XMRV in 40% of QQ patients versus 1/66 heterozygous/wild-type patients, although its own cohort found only rare XMRV and only in non-QQ individuals (PMID: 18823818). Other supportive items are mostly background or review statements noting that XMRV was originally identified in prostate cancer tissue from men with the reduced-activity RNase L variant, without directly quantifying genotype-specific tumor prevalence (PMID: 18684813, PMID: 18950583, PMID: 18382242).
contradict_summary: Most later studies contradict the claim by finding either no XMRV in prostate tumors at all or no association with RNASEL genotype. Large studies from Germany, Australia, Iran, and the US reported zero or extremely low XMRV prevalence across prostate tumors, including among QQ carriers, with some concluding that any positives likely reflected contamination rather than true infection (PMID: 19835577, PMID: 23305518, PMID: 26744630, PMID: 21447170). Other studies detected XMRV in some tumors but found no significant correlation with R462Q, or found that none of the XMRV-positive cancers were QQ, directly opposing genotype enrichment (PMID: 20936978, PMID: 21573232, PMID: 19805305). One Mexican study found no QQ genotype among prostate cancer cases and detected XMRV only in a control sample, so it could not evaluate the claimed QQ-tumor association in cases (PMID: 20576103).
notes: There is a notable discrepancy in the supporting set: PMID: 18823818 is supportive only because it cites earlier familial prostate cancer findings; its own data do not support enrichment in QQ tumors and instead found rare XMRV only in wild-type/heterozygous individuals. PMID: 18684813, PMID: 18950583, and PMID: 18382242 are largely contextual/background rather than direct tests of the claim. In the contradicting set, PMID: 23098452 is mixed: it reports XMRV only in QQ and RQ patients and says QQ was most frequent among positives, but also concludes detection was independent of R462Q and does not clearly show that QQ tumors were much more likely than RQ/RR tumors.

claim: Xenotropic murine leukemia virus-related virus can infect human prostate tissue, with viral nucleic acid and protein detectable in stromal cells adjacent to carcinoma.
support_summary: Supportive studies report XMRV nucleic acid in human prostate specimens, including tumor and sometimes adjacent/normal tissue, by PCR, FISH, or serology-correlated assays, suggesting prostate tissue infection in at least some cohorts (PMID: 20936978, PMID: 20371060, PMID: 23098452, PMID: 25233966, PMID: 17234809). Several mechanistic and in vitro papers further show XMRV can infect human prostate-derived cells, including stromal fibroblasts and epithelial cells, and note that the original description localized virus to stromal fibroblasts surrounding tumors (PMID: 20410264, PMID: 20110097, PMID: 19403677, PMID: 20015990). Reviews also describe XMRV as first identified in prostate cancer tissues and discuss possible stroma-based or epithelial localization in prostate carcinogenesis models (PMID: 20517289, PMID: 21294212, PMID: 18382242). However, most supportive abstracts do not themselves directly document both viral nucleic acid and protein specifically in stromal cells adjacent to carcinoma; they mainly support broader detectability/infectivity in prostate tissue.
contradict_summary: A large body of later evidence found no or only extremely rare XMRV in prostate cancer tissues across multiple countries and cohorts, using PCR, RT-PCR, immunohistochemistry, serology, coculture, or FISH, arguing against a reproducible association with human prostate cancer tissue infection (PMID: 19835577, PMID: 21447170, PMID: 23305518, PMID: 22022417, PMID: 21898167, PMID: 24377628, PMID: 20576103). Multiple papers further concluded that apparent XMRV signals were due to mouse DNA/PCR or reagent contamination, and that patient-derived sequences/integration sites were more consistent with contamination than genuine human infection (PMID: 21352548, PMID: 21171979, PMID: 21171966, PMID: 21876752). The strongest contradictory theme is that XMRV was likely a laboratory-generated recombinant linked to xenograft passaging and not a naturally acquired human pathogen, with re-analysis of original/related prostate materials failing to confirm authentic infection (PMID: 23028701, PMID: 22615748, PMID: 22735937, PMID: 22231291). One partially contradictory study still detected XMRV in prostate cancers but localized protein mainly to malignant epithelium rather than stromal cells adjacent to carcinoma, conflicting with the claim's specific cellular localization (PMID: 19805305).
notes: There is substantial conflict between early positive studies and later contamination-focused analyses. Several items in the SUPPORT group are reviews/background papers or in vitro studies rather than direct tissue-localization evidence, so they only indirectly support the claim. Conversely, PMID: 19805305 appears in the CONTRADICT group but actually supports prostate tissue infection while contradicting the specific stromal-cell localization by reporting mainly malignant epithelial staining. The source claim's precise wording about detectable viral nucleic acid and protein in stromal cells adjacent to carcinoma is most directly tied to the original early reports referenced within later abstracts, but many provided abstracts do not independently restate that exact finding.

claim: Impaired RNase L activity may predispose genetically susceptible individuals to viral infection, and such infection may be related to prostate cancer development.
support_summary: Several reviews and mechanistic studies describe RNase L as a key antiviral effector in the interferon/innate immune pathway, and state that reduced RNase L activity can increase susceptibility to viral infection and possibly tumor development, including prostate cancer (PMID: 18950583, PMID: 18382242, PMID: 17681844). Early XMRV-era studies reported that XMRV was identified in prostate cancers from men carrying the reduced-activity RNASEL R462Q variant, and showed experimentally that XMRV replication is more resistant to interferon when RNase L is deficient, supporting biological plausibility for impaired RNase L facilitating infection (PMID: 17234809, PMID: 18684813, PMID: 20410264). Additional genetic/cell studies found that the R462Q variant has reduced RNase activity and impaired apoptosis, and some RNASEL variants were associated with prostate cancer risk or inflammatory markers, indirectly linking impaired RNase L function to prostate carcinogenesis (PMID: 14583476, PMID: 20576793). Overall, the supporting literature mainly advances a hypothesis that deficient RNase L in genetically susceptible men may permit viral infection, with XMRV proposed as one candidate linked to prostate cancer (PMID: 18382242, PMID: 20517289, PMID: 20717743).
contradict_summary: Multiple later case series using sensitive PCR/serology found either no XMRV or only extremely rare detection in prostate cancer tissues, and concluded there was no association between XMRV/related gammaretroviruses and prostate cancer (PMID: 21573232, PMID: 23305518, PMID: 19835577). One Iranian case-control study also found no XMRV in prostate cancer samples and reported that the RNASEL R462Q variant was not significantly associated with prostate cancer risk in that population (PMID: 26744630). Beyond prostate cancer specifically, a study of several viral infections and putatively virus-related conditions found no significant association between RNASEL codon 462 genotypes and susceptibility to common viral infections, arguing against a broad effect of RNASEL polymorphism on viral susceptibility (PMID: 22356654). The main contradictory theme is that the proposed RNase L-XMRV-prostate cancer link was not reproducibly confirmed across populations or assays.
notes: Much of the supporting evidence consists of reviews, hypothesis-driven interpretations, mechanistic/cell studies, or early XMRV association reports rather than definitive causal human data. There is a major temporal/methodologic discrepancy: early studies/reviews discuss XMRV as a candidate human prostate cancer virus, whereas later studies repeatedly failed to confirm this association and some abstracts explicitly note that XMRV was later considered a laboratory contaminant (PMID: 23305518). A few support-group items are only indirectly relevant to the exact claim because they focus on RNase L biology, apoptosis, or broader cancer susceptibility rather than direct evidence that impaired RNase L predisposes to viral infection causing prostate cancer (e.g., PMID: 14583476, PMID: 20576793, PMID: 18575592, PMID: 26236721).

claim: In Xanthomonas campestris pv. campestris, the HD-GYP domain protein RpfG promotes virulence factor synthesis and biofilm dispersal by degrading cyclic di-GMP.
support_summary: Multiple abstracts support that in Xanthomonas campestris pv. campestris, RpfG is an HD-GYP phosphodiesterase involved in DSF/RpfC signaling that positively regulates virulence-associated traits, including extracellular enzyme/EPS production, motility, and biofilm-related behavior (PMID: 11123673, 17008065, 20231439, 21178479). Several studies state explicitly or infer strongly that RpfG degrades cyclic di-GMP and that this degradation promotes virulence gene expression, including through the global regulator Clp (PMID: 17008065, 20008070, 19220743). Broader analyses of cyclic di-GMP signaling in Xcc also identify RpfG as a key degradation-linked regulator of virulence and virulence factor production (PMID: 17241199, 17153922). One abstract further notes that loss of rpfG causes reduced virulence factors and cell aggregation, consistent with a role in preventing persistent biofilm/attachment states by lowering c-di-GMP (PMID: 21237626).
contradict_summary: The only opposing item does not directly refute the claim; instead, it reports that DSF-mediated regulation of virulence factor production and biofilm dispersal involves a hierarchical network in which Clp is essential for virulence factor regulation but not for biofilm dispersal, while RpfC/RpfG appears to modulate downstream responses by altering c-di-GMP levels (PMID: 17378922). This may qualify the mechanism by suggesting that not all downstream effects of DSF/RpfG on biofilm dispersal are Clp-dependent, rather than contradicting RpfG’s role itself.
notes: The contradiction categorization for PMID: 17378922 appears questionable because its abstract is broadly consistent with RpfC/RpfG affecting virulence factor production and biofilm dispersal via c-di-GMP. Several supporting items are reviews/commentaries rather than primary experiments (PMID: 17153922, 17008065, 21178479), so the strongest abstract-level support for direct phenotypes comes from primary studies such as PMID: 11123673, 20231439, 20008070, 19220743, and 21237626. The exact phrase 'biofilm dispersal' is not explicit in most supporting abstracts; support for that aspect is often indirect via effects on aggregation, biofilm structure, or high-c-di-GMP phenocopies.

claim: Conserved histidine and aspartate residues in the HD-GYP domain are required for both cyclic di-GMP phosphodiesterase activity and regulation of virulence factor synthesis in Xanthomonas campestris pv. campestris.
support_summary: Evidence in Xanthomonas campestris pv. campestris links the RpfG HD-GYP domain to virulence-related signaling, showing that alanine substitution of the conserved GYP motif abolishes RpfG interaction with GGDEF-domain proteins and reduces motility, a virulence-associated trait, while DSF signaling dynamically modulates these interactions (PMID: 20231439). A related discussion article reiterates that conserved GYP motif-dependent RpfG–GGDEF interactions are required for control of a subset of virulence functions and are embedded in cyclic di-GMP signaling pathways (PMID: 21178479). Overall, these studies support a role for conserved HD-GYP-domain residues in regulating virulence-associated functions, especially motility, in Xcc.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
notes: The supporting abstracts focus on the conserved GYP motif and protein-protein interactions, not specifically on the conserved histidine and aspartate residues named in the claim. They also do not directly demonstrate that these residues are required for cyclic di-GMP phosphodiesterase activity; support is therefore indirect/partial rather than a full abstract-level confirmation of both parts of the claim.

claim: The HD-GYP domain functions as a cyclic di-GMP turnover module in bacterial signaling and is broadly distributed among diverse bacteria, including pathogens and beneficial symbionts.
support_summary: Multiple abstracts support that HD-GYP domains act in c-di-GMP turnover, specifically as phosphodiesterases/hydrolases that degrade c-di-GMP (and in some cases to pGpG and/or GMP), in signaling pathways affecting motility, biofilm formation, and virulence across bacteria such as Xanthomonas, Vibrio cholerae, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Bdellovibrio bacteriovorus, Persephonella marina, Bacillus anthracis, Clostridium difficile, and Pseudomonas putida (PMID: 17008065, 17241199, 19170727, 21990613, 24176013, 25343965, 31691501, 34096779). Several studies further place HD-GYP proteins directly in bacterial signaling networks, including quorum sensing/DSF-dependent pathways and dynamic interactions with GGDEF and PilZ proteins that regulate virulence or motility, especially in Xanthomonas spp. (PMID: 17020586, 20231439, 22924852). Broader reviews and phylogenetic/domain analyses explicitly describe HD-GYP as a c-di-GMP phosphodiesterase family involved in signal transduction and discuss its phylogenetic distribution and varied domain architectures across bacteria (PMID: 17153922, 23704785, 28031279, 34928179, 32496757, 22593024). Overall, the evidence strongly supports the functional part of the claim and provides moderate support that HD-GYP is distributed across diverse bacteria, including multiple pathogens and at least nonpathogenic/predatory environmental species such as Bdellovibrio and Persephonella (PMID: 21990613, 24176013, 34928179).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
notes: A recurring limitation is that many abstracts demonstrate HD-GYP function in c-di-GMP turnover but do not directly address breadth of distribution, and few explicitly mention beneficial symbionts; thus support for the 'including beneficial symbionts' portion is indirect rather than explicit. Some evidence items are reviews rather than primary experiments, and at least some abstracts emphasize functional diversification or additional substrates/interactions beyond c-di-GMP, which does not negate the main claim but narrows how universally the turnover role should be interpreted. No clear support/contradict categorization conflicts were evident from the provided abstracts.

claim: In human breast and head and neck cancers, hypoxia-associated lysyl oxidase expression is linked to increased metastatic risk and poorer survival outcomes.
support_summary: Multiple studies support that hypoxia induces LOX in breast cancer and that this promotes invasion, premetastatic niche formation, and metastasis, including bone relapse in ER-negative disease (PMID: 17685448, PMID: 19111879, PMID: 21911388, PMID: 26017313). In breast cancer, LOX expression has also been linked to reduced survival or poor prognosis in patient datasets and tumor samples, with high LOX/low GATA3 associated with poor prognosis and higher LOX correlating with decreased metastasis-free and overall survival (PMID: 17685448, PMID: 21892208, PMID: 19440335). For head and neck cancer, a prospective validation study showed that the hypoxia-related protein LOX independently predicted time to metastasis, progression, and overall survival in locally advanced HNC, with substantially higher metastatic risk at higher LOX levels (PMID: 19667273). Additional oral/head and neck evidence reports elevated LOX in OSCC with lymph node metastasis and death of disease, and experimental oral carcinoma work found hypoxia-regulated LOX contributes to invasion patterns (PMID: 32744444, PMID: 23262025).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
notes: Much of the supporting set consists of reviews, mechanistic studies, or animal/preclinical models rather than direct human outcome studies, so the strongest human clinical support comes mainly from head and neck cancer (PMID: 19667273) and some breast cancer correlative studies (PMID: 17685448, PMID: 19440335). Several articles support only parts of the claim (for example breast metastasis mechanisms without survival data, or LOX prognosis without explicit hypoxia linkage), but no clear abstract-level evidence here contradicts the claim.

claim: In mice with orthotopically grown breast cancer tumors, inhibiting lysyl oxidase prevents metastasis.
support_summary: Direct support comes from an orthotopic breast cancer rodent model in which either a small-molecule LOX inhibitor or an anti-LOX antibody abolished metastasis, closely matching the claim (PMID: 17079439). Additional breast cancer studies show that LOX inhibition or knockdown reduces metastatic niche formation, bone marrow-derived cell recruitment, metastatic growth, and lung metastasis in orthotopic models, including effects on LOX family members and upstream regulators of LOX activity (PMID: 19111879, PMID: 21911388, PMID: 22231744, PMID: 30890638). Other in vivo work in breast cancer or broader tumor models also reports reduced metastatic colonization, micrometastasis, or premetastatic bone lesion formation after LOX inhibition, though these are less directly matched to the exact orthotopic primary-tumor claim (PMID: 19440335, PMID: 26017313, PMID: 24045659, PMID: 23345161). Overall, the recurring theme is that LOX promotes extracellular-matrix remodeling and premetastatic niche formation, and blocking LOX activity suppresses metastasis.
contradict_summary: One study reported that knockdown of LOXL4, a lysyl oxidase family member, increased primary tumor growth and lung metastasis in a triple-negative breast cancer xenograft model, suggesting this paralog may have context-dependent anti-metastatic effects (PMID: 28060764). This does not directly test inhibition of LOX itself in orthotopic breast tumors, but it does indicate that not all LOX-family inhibition can be assumed to reduce metastasis.
notes: Most supporting items are directionally consistent, but several are reviews, mechanistic papers, or indirect studies targeting HIF or ATP7A rather than directly inhibiting LOX itself, so they are weaker support for the exact claim. The lone contradictory item concerns LOXL4 rather than LOX and appears to address a different family member and model, so its relevance to the specific claim is limited. There is a support/contradict categorization discrepancy risk because some included papers are broad reviews rather than primary direct evidence.

claim: In hypoxic human cancer cells, secreted lysyl oxidase promotes invasion through focal adhesion kinase activity and enhanced cell-matrix adhesion, supporting formation of a metastatic niche.
support_summary: Multiple studies support that hypoxia induces LOX expression/secretion and that LOX promotes tumor invasion/migration, with knockdown or inhibition reducing these phenotypes in several cancer models, including NSCLC and breast cancer (PMID: 23140307, PMID: 16357151, PMID: 22434522). Mechanistically, abstracts report LOX-dependent activation of focal adhesion signaling, including FAK/Src and paxillin phosphorylation, together with increased cell-matrix adhesion or migration (PMID: 16357151, PMID: 17685448, PMID: 22434522, PMID: 24045659, PMID: 17931358). Several studies also support the metastatic niche aspect, showing that hypoxic tumor-derived LOX crosslinks collagen at distant sites, recruits CD11b+ bone marrow/myeloid cells, and promotes lung/bone premetastatic niche formation and metastasis (PMID: 19111879, PMID: 21911388, PMID: 22231744, PMID: 25238333, PMID: 30890638). Additional hypoxia-focused reviews and disease-specific studies are broadly consistent with a role for secreted LOX in invasion and metastatic niche remodeling, though often without detailing the FAK/adhesion mechanism (PMID: 17079439, PMID: 22921864, PMID: 30862717).
contradict_summary: No abstract directly refutes the claim that hypoxia-induced LOX promotes invasion; the one hypoxic lung cancer study placed in the contradict set actually found that LOX knockdown reduced invasion/migration and increased E-cadherin, which supports the claim rather than contradicting it (PMID: 23328296). The remaining opposing papers concern the LOX propeptide (LOX-PP), a distinct cleavage product with tumor-suppressive effects that inhibits migration, invasion, EMT-related signaling, or transformed phenotype in several models (PMID: 17616686, PMID: 24573150, PMID: 17283144). These LOX-PP findings do not directly negate pro-metastatic functions of mature/secreted LOX because they address a different molecular species and mechanism.
notes: There is a clear curation discrepancy: PMID: 23328296 is misclassified as contradicting, since its abstract supports a pro-invasive role for LOX under hypoxia. Also, several support items are reviews/commentaries or focus on related contexts (e.g., tumor endothelial cells, surgery, radiotherapy, bone niche) rather than directly testing hypoxic human cancer cells, FAK activity, and cell-matrix adhesion in the same experiment; they should be interpreted as indirect support. The strongest direct mechanistic support for FAK/adhesion comes from breast cancer and astrocyte studies (PMID: 16357151, PMID: 17685448, PMID: 22434522, PMID: 17931358), whereas the strongest support for metastatic niche formation comes from hypoxia/LOX premetastatic niche studies (PMID: 19111879, PMID: 21911388, PMID: 22231744, PMID: 25238333).

claim: In mice lacking both proapoptotic BCL-2 family members BAX and BAK, endoplasmic reticulum stress induced by tunicamycin caused impaired unfolded protein response signaling in the liver, with extensive tissue damage and reduced IRE1alpha-dependent gene expression.
support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
notes: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

claim: In cells lacking both BAX and BAK, endoplasmic reticulum stress led to deficient IRE1alpha signaling compared with cells expressing BAX and BAK.
support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
notes: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

claim: BAX and BAK are required for activation of IRE1alpha signaling during endoplasmic reticulum stress, indicating that proapoptotic BCL-2 family members provide a functional link between apoptosis regulators and the unfolded protein response.
support_summary: Supportive evidence is indirect but consistent with a role for proapoptotic BCL-2 family members in IRE1α signaling. One study states that BAX and BAK localize to the ER, directly interact with IRE1α, and promote its activity, while showing that BCL-2 overexpression enhances XBP1 splicing and thus modulates this pathway (PMID: 26319553). Another study in lung injury/fibrosis reports that IRE1α activation depends on its physical association with BAX, and that BCL-2 disrupts this association to attenuate ER stress, supporting a functional link between BCL-2 family proteins and the UPR (PMID: 38117250).
contradict_summary: The contradicting evidence does not directly refute the claim that BAX/BAK are required for IRE1α activation. In HeLa cells exposed to subtilase cytotoxin, IRE1α, JNK, and CHOP signaling were not required for Bax/Bak-mediated mitochondrial permeabilization and apoptosis, but this addresses whether IRE1α is upstream of Bax/Bak in apoptosis rather than whether Bax/Bak are required for IRE1α signaling (PMID: 20561923). In experimental pancreatitis, ANP increased Bax and Bak while not affecting IRE1/sXBP1 signaling, suggesting these proteins can change independently of IRE1 output in that context, but this does not test necessity of BAX/BAK for IRE1α activation (PMID: 30529145).
notes: Both 'contradict' papers appear only tangentially related to the claim and may be miscategorized: PMID: 20561923 examines whether IRE1α/JNK/CHOP are needed for Bax/Bak-dependent apoptosis, not whether Bax/Bak activate IRE1α, and PMID: 30529145 reports no change in IRE1/sXBP1 despite altered Bax/Bak levels but does not test causality. The supporting studies are also partial rather than definitive from the abstracts alone, because PMID: 26319553 largely cites prior demonstration of BAX/BAK-IRE1α interaction and PMID: 38117250 emphasizes BAX specifically, not clearly BAK.

claim: A lung metagene gene-expression model predicted recurrence risk in patients with early-stage non-small-cell lung cancer better than clinical prognostic factors and showed consistent performance across early disease stages.
support_summary: Several studies support that gene-expression assays can outperform standard clinical or staging-based prognostic factors in early-stage NSCLC. In prospective cohorts, a 14-gene assay showed better risk stratification than NCCN high-risk clinicopathologic criteria, with recurrence or DFS outcomes more closely tracking molecular risk than clinical risk groups (PMID: 25258195; PMID: 28645632). Other validation studies reported that genomic signatures remained independently prognostic after adjustment for clinical factors and worked across early-stage subsets including stage IA/I/II or stage I-II disease, suggesting consistency across early stages (PMID: 24305008; PMID: 22047960; PMID: 21242119). Additional studies of other NSCLC gene signatures also found significant prediction of recurrence-free or overall survival in early-stage cohorts, reinforcing the broader theme that molecular profiling adds prognostic information beyond traditional classification (PMID: 19118056; PMID: 19118056; PMID: 25301404; PMID: 19176396).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
notes: Most supporting papers evaluate related gene-expression signatures rather than the exact original 'lung metagene' model from the source claim, so they provide indirect rather than model-specific confirmation. Several studies focus on overall survival or mortality rather than recurrence specifically, and some do not directly compare against clinical prognostic factors, which limits how directly they address the claim (e.g., PMID: 24305008, PMID: 17194181, PMID: 34435876). One citation appears duplicated in substance in the summary list (PMID: 19118056).

claim: A lung metagene gene-expression model identified a subgroup of patients with stage IA non-small-cell lung cancer who were at high risk of recurrence after surgery.
support_summary: Multiple studies support the general claim that tumor gene-expression signatures can identify surgically resected early-stage NSCLC patients, including stage I/IA subsets, who are at higher risk of poor outcomes or recurrence. Independent validation studies showed prognostic classifiers stratifying stage IA patients into high- and low-risk groups, including a 15-gene signature with significant prognostic separation in stage IA NSCLC (PMID: 24305008), and other signatures predicting recurrence or survival in stage I disease after resection (PMID: 19118056, PMID: 21242119, PMID: 17194181, PMID: 22047960). More specifically for recurrence after surgery, gene-expression assays identified high-risk early-stage NSCLC or squamous carcinoma subgroups with worse recurrence-free outcomes and potential utility for adjuvant treatment selection (PMID: 25258195, PMID: 28645632, PMID: 27613525). Review articles also describe gene-expression profiling as improving prognostic staging beyond conventional clinicopathologic criteria in early-stage NSCLC (PMID: 34435876, PMID: 38013359, PMID: 22263026, PMID: 15552795).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
notes: Most supporting studies do not directly evaluate the exact original 'lung metagene' model from the source claim and instead assess other gene-expression classifiers, so support is indirect rather than model-specific. Several abstracts focus on overall survival or mortality rather than recurrence specifically (for example PMID: 24305008, PMID: 17194181), and some are limited to histologic subtypes such as adenocarcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma rather than all stage IA NSCLC (PMID: 22047960, PMID: 27613525, PMID: 34028782). No major discrepancies within the provided evidence set were identified, but these scope differences should be considered when comparing to the original claim.

claim: A lung metagene gene-expression model could help refine prognosis and potentially guide decisions about adjuvant chemotherapy in early-stage non-small-cell lung cancer.
support_summary: Multiple studies support that gene-expression signatures can refine prognosis beyond conventional staging in early-stage NSCLC, including stage I disease, by identifying patients at higher risk of recurrence or death after resection (PMID: 17194181, PMID: 19118056, PMID: 24305008, PMID: 21242119). Several reports also suggest these models may help guide adjuvant chemotherapy decisions: a 14-gene assay prospectively stratified risk better than NCCN high-risk criteria and showed markedly better 5-year DFS in molecular high-risk patients who received adjuvant chemotherapy versus those who did not (PMID: 28645632), while physicians changed chemotherapy recommendations in about 31% of tested early-stage cases after receiving 14-gene assay results (PMID: 24902933). Additional predictive signatures linked gene-expression-defined high-risk groups to survival benefit from adjuvant chemotherapy in independent datasets or trial-derived cohorts (PMID: 22157961, PMID: 23357979, PMID: 27502323). Broadly, the recurring theme is that molecular classifiers may outperform clinicopathologic factors alone and support more individualized postoperative management in early-stage NSCLC (PMID: 22047960, PMID: 25258195, PMID: 34435876).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
notes: Most evidence is supportive but heterogeneous, spanning prognostic-only signatures, predictive signatures for chemotherapy benefit, physician decision-impact studies, reviews, and cost-effectiveness analyses rather than randomized trials. Several studies focus on specific histologies such as adenocarcinoma or squamous carcinoma, or on related biomarkers rather than the exact lung metagene model from the source article, so support is often conceptual rather than a direct replication of the same model. Some abstracts only support improved prognostic stratification and imply, rather than directly demonstrate, chemotherapy guidance.

claim: In rodent hepatocellular carcinomas, hepatic miR-122 expression is downregulated relative to normal liver tissue, indicating an association between miR-122 loss and hepatocarcinogenesis.
support_summary: Multiple rodent liver cancer models report reduced hepatic miR-122 during hepatocarcinogenesis. In a CDAA diet mouse model, miR-122 was downregulated at early stages of hepatocarcinogenesis relative to control diet livers, supporting an association between miR-122 loss and liver tumor development (PMID: 19711427). In mouse liver tumors driven by c-Myc or AKT/Ras, miR-122 was among the downregulated miRNAs, and its re-expression showed weak to moderate tumor suppressor effects, consistent with a functional role in suppressing hepatocarcinogenesis (PMID: 25762642). Additional support for the biological premise comes from a study reporting that miR-122 is downregulated in most HCCs and that restoring miR-122 suppresses HCC growth in vitro and in mouse xenografts, although this study is not clearly a rodent primary tumor comparison (PMID: 24244539).
contradict_summary: One study reported the opposite pattern, finding miR-122 overexpressed in hepatic tumors relative to normal liver parenchyma (PMID: 18307259). However, this evidence comes from hepatitis C virus-associated human dysplastic nodules and hepatocellular carcinomas rather than rodent tumors, so it contradicts the direction of expression change but is only indirectly applicable to the rodent-specific claim.
notes: The main supportive theme is consistent downregulation of miR-122 in mouse hepatocarcinogenesis models, with some functional evidence that restoring miR-122 suppresses tumor phenotypes. A key discrepancy is that PMID: 24244539 discusses HCC broadly and mouse xenografts after miR-122 restoration, but the abstract does not clearly establish a rodent primary tumor vs normal liver expression comparison. The sole contradicting paper (PMID: 18307259) is misaligned with the claim context because it studies human HCV-associated tumors, not rodent HCC; this species/context mismatch should temper how strongly it is weighed.

claim: In human primary hepatocellular carcinomas, miR-122 expression is reduced in tumor tissue relative to paired non-tumor liver tissue, supporting miR-122 downregulation as a feature of human liver cancer.
support_summary: Multiple studies support that miR-122 is reduced in human HCC. A genome-wide miRNA study reported hepatocyte-specific miR-122a was down-regulated in about 70% of primary HCCs, with validation by Northern blot and qRT-PCR, and showed inverse correlation with cyclin G1 in primary liver carcinomas (PMID: 17616664). Another study states miR-122 is significantly down-regulated in most HCCs while functionally acting as a tumor suppressor through AKT3 targeting (PMID: 24244539). Additional indirect support comes from matched human HCC/benign liver Ago-CLIP work describing molecular consequences of miR-122 downregulation in HCC (PMID: 28735896), whereas one study only shows miR-122 downregulation in the HepG2 cell line rather than primary human tumors (PMID: 27687586).
contradict_summary: One study of hepatitis C virus-associated primary liver tumors contradicts the claim, reporting that miR-122 was overexpressed in hepatic tumors relative to normal liver parenchyma in an extended set including 43 HCCs and 9 dysplastic nodules (PMID: 18307259). This suggests that at least in this HCV-associated cohort, miR-122 may not be uniformly reduced in tumor tissue.
notes: The overall evidence trend favors miR-122 downregulation in HCC, but there is heterogeneity across cohorts and disease contexts. The contradicting study used normal liver as calibrator rather than explicitly paired adjacent non-tumor tissue and included dysplastic nodules as well as HCCs, which is not identical to the claim's paired tumor/non-tumor comparison (PMID: 18307259). PMID: 27687586 is only indirect evidence because it analyzes an HCC cell line, not primary human tumor versus paired non-tumor tissue. PMID: 28735896 also supports the concept of downregulation but does not clearly quantify expression differences from the abstract alone.

claim: Downregulation of miR-122 appears to be tumor-specific in hepatocarcinoma and may serve as a biomarker for liver cancers.
support_summary: Multiple studies report that the liver-specific miR-122/miR-122a is frequently reduced in hepatocellular carcinoma, including about 70% of primary HCCs and all tested HCC-derived cell lines in one study, supporting a recurring association with liver tumor tissue (PMID: 17616664). Functional studies further describe miR-122 as a tumor suppressor in HCC, with restoration reducing proliferation, migration, xenograft growth, or oncogenic targets such as AKT3, WNT1, and cyclin G1, linking its loss to hepatocarcinogenesis (PMID: 24244539; PMID: 27687586; PMID: 17616664). Additional evidence from mouse hepatocarcinogenesis models and matched human HCC/benign liver analyses shows miR-122 downregulation during early liver tumor development and broad molecular consequences in HCC, including associations with patient survival-related target networks (PMID: 19711427; PMID: 28735896). Overall, the abstracts support that miR-122 downregulation is common in HCC and biologically relevant, but most do not directly establish tumor-specificity or validate it explicitly as a clinical biomarker.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
notes: There is a discrepancy within the supplied evidence: one abstract reports that miR-122, miR-100, and miR-10a were overexpressed in HCV-associated hepatic tumors relative to normal liver, which conflicts with the claim and suggests this item may be miscategorized as support (PMID: 18307259). Across the rest of the evidence, support is mainly for frequent downregulation and tumor-suppressive function of miR-122 in HCC, whereas the stronger parts of the claim—"tumor-specific" downregulation and use as a biomarker—are only indirectly addressed in the abstracts.

claim: Gene expression signatures can predict sensitivity to individual chemotherapeutic drugs in cancer cells and can also predict clinical response in patients treated with those drugs.
support_summary: Multiple studies report that baseline gene-expression profiles from cancer cell lines, xenografts, or tumors can generate drug-specific predictors of chemosensitivity, including for taxanes, ixabepilone, platinum, anthracyclines, 5-FU/gemcitabine, and other agents, with internal or external validation in several settings (PMID: 17878523, PMID: 21792624, PMID: 17290060, PMID: 29026541). Several patient-based studies further show that pretreatment or treatment-induced expression signatures can distinguish responders from nonresponders or predict pathologic response, time to progression, progression-free survival, or survival after chemotherapy, including breast, gastric, ovarian, and lymphoma cohorts (PMID: 25886164, PMID: 28511180, PMID: 19809382, PMID: 25881228). Recurring themes are that some signatures are regimen- or drug-specific rather than generic prognostic markers, and that cross-validation or external validation can support clinical predictive value (PMID: 21792624, PMID: 25886164, PMID: 17290060). Additional supportive evidence comes from studies linking pathway or single-gene expression markers to in vitro sensitivity and clinical response, though these are narrower than multigene signature claims (PMID: 16273092, PMID: 25480692, PMID: 22481060).
contradict_summary: One breast cancer study found that established prognostic gene-expression signatures from prior literature did not distinguish responders from nonresponders to neoadjuvant TAC chemotherapy, indicating that prognostic signatures are not automatically predictive of chemotherapy response and that therapy-specific classifiers may be required (PMID: 16673249). A retraction notice exists for a paper on genomic signatures to guide chemotherapeutic use, which weakens confidence in at least one prior report but does not itself provide substantive contrary data from an abstract (PMID: 21217686).
notes: Some supporting items are reviews, perspective pieces, or abstracts truncated/lacking detail, so they provide indirect rather than direct evidence for the exact claim (e.g., PMID: 18020912, PMID: 18387932, PMID: 31932791, PMID: 28649435). Several studies focus on combination regimens, targeted agents, pathway signatures, or single biomarkers rather than prediction for an individual cytotoxic drug, so they only partially match the claim (e.g., PMID: 25922429, PMID: 25480692, PMID: 21769435). The main discrepancy across studies is not whether expression-based predictors can exist, but that generic prognostic signatures may fail while drug- or regimen-specific signatures perform better (PMID: 16673249).

claim: Gene expression signatures developed for individual chemotherapeutic agents can be combined to predict response to multidrug chemotherapy regimens.
support_summary: Direct support comes from a study that built resistance gene-expression signatures for individual R-CHOP components and the CHO combination using cell lines, then showed the combination signature had prognostic value in diffuse large B-cell lymphoma patients treated with R-CHOP, demonstrating feasibility of predicting multidrug-regimen response from pharmacogenomic modeling (PMID: 25881228). Additional indirect support comes from work showing that gene-expression responses to single agents can forecast the effect of drug combinations, because concordance between single-drug transcriptional responses predicted whether combinations produced greater or lesser-than-expected effects in colorectal cancer cells (PMID: 33093925). Another in vitro study identified baseline genes associated with synergistic response to taxane plus SAHA, suggesting gene-expression biomarkers can inform combination-therapy response, though it did not explicitly combine prebuilt single-agent signatures (PMID: 20217129).
contradict_summary: One evidence item is a retraction notice for a paper on genomic signatures to guide chemotherapy use, which raises concerns about reliability of some prior signature-based chemotherapy prediction work, but the abstract provides no direct details on the specific claim about combining individual-agent signatures for multidrug regimens (PMID: 21217686). Another breast cancer neoadjuvant study found that established prognostic gene-expression signatures could not distinguish responders from non-responders to TAC chemotherapy, arguing that generic prognostic signatures are insufficient for therapy-response prediction and that treatment-specific classifiers are needed (PMID: 16673249). Overall, the contradiction is indirect: it challenges robustness or transferability of some signatures rather than directly disproving the combination-of-individual-agent-signatures approach.
notes: PMID: 31932791 has no abstract here, so it cannot meaningfully support the claim from abstract-level evidence alone. PMID: 20217129 and PMID: 33093925 are only indirect support because they address biomarkers or transcriptional concordance for drug combinations rather than explicitly combining independently derived single-agent response signatures in patients. PMID: 16673249 evaluates prognostic signatures, not signatures developed for individual chemotherapeutic agents, so its contradiction is only partial. The retraction item (PMID: 21217686) is relevant as a cautionary signal but lacks abstract content and may reflect problems with a specific publication rather than the general claim.

claim: Integrating chemotherapy response gene expression signatures with signatures of oncogenic pathway deregulation can identify new therapeutic strategies that improve use of available anticancer drugs, including combinations with targeted therapies.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from an ovarian cancer study showing that a gene-expression predictor of platinum resistance, combined with oncogenic pathway signatures, identified Src and Rb/E2F pathway activation in resistant tumors and suggested targeted interventions that increased chemotherapy response in cell lines (PMID: 17290060). Broader studies and reviews report that pathway-deregulation signatures can predict sensitivity to targeted agents and that, when considered alongside chemotherapy-response or clinicogenomic signatures, they can refine prognosis and help guide cytotoxic and targeted therapy selection in breast cancer and NSCLC (PMID: 16273092, PMID: 18387932, PMID: 18020912). Additional indirect support comes from studies identifying expression signatures predictive of benefit from targeted combinations such as bevacizumab/erlotinib and from gene-expression approaches to finding synergistic drug combinations or biomarkers of taxane/SAHA response (PMID: 25922429, PMID: 20217129, PMID: 33093925).
contradict_summary: The only contradicting item is a retraction notice for a paper on genomic signatures guiding chemotherapeutic use (PMID: 21217686). From the available information, this undermines at least one prior report in this area, but the abstract provides no details showing that the broader claim about integrating chemotherapy-response and pathway-deregulation signatures is false.
notes: Most supporting items are indirect or conceptual rather than direct demonstrations of the exact integrated strategy in the claim; PMID: 17290060 is the clearest direct match. The contradicting evidence is limited to a retraction notice without substantive abstract data, so it weakens confidence in some literature but does not by itself establish contradiction of the whole claim. PMID: 31932791 has no abstract here, so its relevance is uncertain. Some SUPPORT items focus mainly on pathway signatures or drug-combination expression effects rather than explicit integration with chemotherapy-response signatures, so they should be interpreted cautiously.

claim: Mice with a loss-of-function mutation in Toll-like receptor 4 are protected against diet-induced obesity compared with control mice during high-fat feeding.
support_summary: Several studies directly report reduced obesity or weight gain in TLR4-deficient mice during high-fat feeding. Tlr4-deficient 10ScN mice were completely protected from obesity induced by a high saturated fat/palmitate diet, with reduced adipose macrophage infiltration and MCP-1 responses (PMID: 18421279). TLR4 loss-of-function or knockout also protected against diet-induced obesity in studies focused on hypothalamic inflammation and ER stress, including reduced HFD-induced body weight gain and glucose intolerance in TLR4-/- mice (PMID: 19144836, PMID: 23741455). Additional supporting items mostly emphasize attenuation of obesity-related fibrosis, oxidative stress, or cite prior work on HFD-induced obesity protection rather than directly demonstrating lower adiposity in the abstract (PMID: 24794440, PMID: 31950846, PMID: 28776958).
contradict_summary: Multiple abstracts indicate that TLR4 deficiency improves inflammatory or insulin-resistance phenotypes without clearly preventing obesity itself. In one study, TLR4-/- and wild-type mice had similar increases in body weight and body fat on a saturated high-fat diet, despite protection from vascular inflammation and insulin resistance in the knockout animals (PMID: 17478729). Female C57BL/6 mice lacking TLR4 were reported to have increased obesity but partial protection from HFD-induced insulin resistance (PMID: 17053832), and hematopoietic-specific Tlr4 deletion improved insulin sensitivity even though mice still became obese on HFD (PMID: 19883619). Another study suggests RP105/MD-1 deficiency produced less HFD-induced inflammation and steatosis than TLR4 knockout mice, implying TLR4 loss was not the most protective model in that context (PMID: 22396206).
notes: The evidence is mixed and appears context-dependent, varying by mouse strain/sex, tissue specificity, and especially diet composition; the strongest direct support is for protection against saturated-fat-driven obesity rather than all high-fat diets. PMID: 17426960 is discrepant with the support grouping because its abstract reports no significant body-weight reduction and even higher epididymal fat mass/adipocyte hypertrophy in TLR4-mutant mice, so it does not directly support protection from obesity. PMID: 28776958 and PMID: 31950846 provide indirect/background support rather than primary direct evidence on the claim from the abstract. PMID: 22396206 is only indirectly contradictory because it compares TLR4 KO with RP105/MD-1 KO rather than directly with wild-type for obesity outcomes.

claim: Mice with a loss-of-function mutation in Toll-like receptor 4 have improved insulin sensitivity and enhanced insulin-signaling capacity in adipose tissue, muscle, and liver during high-fat feeding compared with control mice.
support_summary: Several studies report that loss or deficiency of TLR4 improves metabolic responses to high-fat feeding, especially in adipose tissue and liver. TLR4-mutant or deficient mice showed reduced adipose inflammation with improved adipose glucose transport/insulin sensitivity and less hepatic steatosis during fat-enriched diets (PMID: 17426960), while hematopoietic or hepatocyte-specific TLR4 deletion improved hepatic and adipose insulin sensitivity, glucose tolerance, and inflammatory profiles in obese mice (PMID: 19883619, PMID: 24815961). Additional support comes from studies showing protection from lipid- or HFD-induced impairment of insulin signaling/systemic glucose metabolism, including muscle or vascular insulin responsiveness (PMID: 17053832, PMID: 17478729), and from reports of improved liver Akt signaling or partial protection from peripheral insulin resistance in specialized HFD models (PMID: 20860587, PMID: 26539824). Overall, the recurring theme is that TLR4 loss dampens obesity-related inflammation and often preserves insulin action, though many abstracts support only some of the tissues named in the claim rather than adipose, muscle, and liver together.
contradict_summary: Contradictory studies indicate that TLR4 deficiency does not consistently improve insulin sensitivity during high-fat feeding. One study found that despite reduced adipose inflammation and altered macrophage polarization, neither global nor hematopoietic TLR4 deficiency changed systemic insulin sensitivity after high-fat feeding (PMID: 22751700). Another reported that C3H/HeJ TLR4-mutant mice were not protected against HFD-induced insulin resistance even though adipose inflammation was reduced, suggesting adipocyte hypertrophy can drive insulin resistance independently of inflammation (PMID: 25733684). More limited conflicting evidence comes from adipocyte-specific TLR4 deletion, which worsened whole-body and muscle insulin resistance during chronic HFD despite benefit in an acute lipid challenge (PMID: 28323977), and from a study implying TLR4 knockout mice were less protected than RP105/MD-1 knockout mice against HFD-induced insulin resistance (PMID: 22396206).
notes: The evidence is mixed and tissue/context dependent. The strongest support for the exact claim appears indirect from this set, because many supporting abstracts document benefits in adipose tissue and liver, or in vascular/cardiac/systemic phenotypes, but do not explicitly demonstrate enhanced insulin-signaling capacity across adipose tissue, muscle, and liver simultaneously. There is also heterogeneity by model (global mutation/knockout vs hepatocyte-, adipocyte-, or hematopoietic-specific deletion), diet composition (general HFD vs saturated-fat enriched diets), sex, and duration of feeding, which likely explains some discrepancies. PMID: 22396206 is only indirectly contradictory because it mainly compares RP105/MD-1 knockout with TLR4 knockout rather than directly showing absence of benefit versus wild-type controls.

claim: Muscle from mice with a loss-of-function mutation in Toll-like receptor 4 is protected against saturated fatty acid-induced insulin resistance compared with muscle from control mice.
support_summary: Evidence supports a role for TLR4 in lipid/fat-induced insulin resistance, though most studies are indirect relative to the specific ex vivo muscle claim. In mice lacking TLR4, systemic lipid infusion was reported to no longer suppress muscle insulin signaling and to have less impact on whole-body glucose metabolism, supporting protection from fatty acid-related insulin resistance (PMID: 17053832). Longer-term in vivo studies also found partial protection of TLR4-mutant or TLR4-knockout mice from high-fat diet-induced insulin resistance/glucose intolerance, including effects involving skeletal muscle and peripheral metabolism (PMID: 26539824, PMID: 23741455).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
notes: The available evidence mainly concerns systemic lipid infusion or high-fat diet models rather than a direct test of isolated muscle exposed to saturated fatty acids, so support is mechanistically and physiologically relevant but not perfectly matched to the claim. PMID: 26539824 emphasizes peripheral/cardiac metabolic effects, and PMID: 23741455 focuses more on ER stress and glucose intolerance than direct muscle insulin resistance. No major discrepancy between grouping and abstract content was evident, but the evidence is broader than the precise statement.

claim: In cardiomyocytes exposed to oxidative stress, miR-1 promotes apoptosis by post-transcriptionally reducing HSP60 and HSP70 expression.
support_summary: The supporting studies consistently indicate that miR-1 promotes cardiomyocyte injury/apoptosis while post-transcriptionally suppressing HSP60, with direct targeting shown in ischemia-reperfusion models and high-glucose settings (PMID: 23226300, PMID: 20655308). One cited study specifically reports opposing effects of miR-1 and miR-133 on apoptosis through targeting HSP60, HSP70, and caspase-9 in cardiomyocytes, aligning most closely with the claim’s HSP60/HSP70 mechanism (PMID: 21914820). Overall, the evidence supports a pro-apoptotic role for miR-1 in cardiomyocytes mediated by reduced heat-shock protein expression, especially HSP60.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
notes: Evidence strength is uneven for the full claim: PMID: 20655308 and PMID: 23226300 support miR-1-driven apoptosis via post-transcriptional HSP60 reduction, but their abstracts do not specifically mention oxidative stress or HSP70. PMID: 21914820 appears highly relevant to HSP60/HSP70 targeting, but no abstract text was provided here, so conclusions from that study should be treated cautiously. Thus, support is strongest for miR-1 reducing HSP60 and promoting apoptosis in cardiomyocytes, while direct abstract-level support for the exact oxidative-stress context and HSP70 component is limited in the provided evidence.

claim: In cardiomyocytes exposed to oxidative stress, miR-133 inhibits apoptosis by post-transcriptionally reducing caspase-9 expression.
support_summary: Support comes from studies in cardiomyocytes showing that higher miR-133 is associated with less apoptosis and lower caspase-9 under stress conditions. In H2O2-treated cardiomyocytes, miR-133 overexpression mimicked carvedilol’s protection and reduced caspase-9/caspase-3 expression while decreasing apoptosis, consistent with post-transcriptional repression of the apoptotic pathway (PMID: 25066695). Additional support is provided by reports that Tanshinone IIA upregulates miR-133 while suppressing caspase-9 in H9c2 cells (PMID: 28867607), and by a study identifying caspase-9 among miR-133 targets in cardiomyocytes with anti-apoptotic effects of miR-133 (PMID: 21914820).
contradict_summary: No true contradicting evidence is evident from the abstract provided. The nicotine study actually reports that miR-133 is downregulated during cardiomyocyte apoptosis and that caspase-9, described as a miR-133 target, is correspondingly upregulated, which is directionally consistent with the claim rather than contradictory (PMID: 24190542).
notes: There is a clear discrepancy in the CONTRADICT grouping: PMID: 24190542 appears miscategorized because its abstract supports an inverse relationship between miR-133 and caspase-9 during cardiomyocyte apoptosis. Evidence strength varies because some abstracts are truncated or sparse, especially PMID: 28867607 and PMID: 21914820, so mechanistic details such as direct post-transcriptional targeting are less explicit there than in PMID: 25066695.

claim: In cardiomyocytes, increased miR-1 activity and decreased miR-133 activity favor cell death, whereas decreased miR-1 activity and decreased miR-133 activity favor cell survival.
support_summary: Multiple studies support a pro-apoptotic role for miR-1 in cardiomyocytes: miR-1 overexpression increased apoptosis/injury, while miR-1 inhibition was protective in oxidative stress or ischemia-reperfusion settings (PMID: 19506341, PMID: 23226300). Several studies also support an anti-apoptotic role for miR-133, showing that higher miR-133 reduced cardiomyocyte apoptosis and that loss of miR-133 increased caspase-9/caspase-3 signaling and cell death; this was seen with carvedilol, Tanshinone IIA, and nicotine-related models (PMID: 25066695, PMID: 28867607, PMID: 24190542). A directly relevant study reported that miR-1 and miR-133 have opposing effects on cardiomyocyte apoptosis via HSP60, HSP70, and caspase-9, consistent with miR-1 promoting and miR-133 restraining cell death (PMID: 21914820). Another recent report is broadly consistent in linking regulation of miR-1 with reduced oxidative damage/apoptosis, though the truncated abstract limits specifics (PMID: 35179668).
contradict_summary: One study in diabetic cardiomyocytes found that both miR-1 and miR-133a/b were downregulated in association with diabetic cardiac dysfunction and oxidative stress, and that antioxidant treatment restored their expression along with cardioprotection (PMID: 23723006). From the abstract alone, this pattern does not fit a simple model in which decreased miR-1 favors survival, because lower miR-1 accompanied injury rather than protection. However, this study focuses on diabetic remodeling/cardiac dysfunction and does not directly test apoptosis outcomes from manipulating miR-1 versus miR-133 in the way the claim states.
notes: There is a key discrepancy within the claim itself: the phrase 'decreased miR-1 activity and decreased miR-133 activity favor cell survival' conflicts with the broader evidence base, because decreased miR-133 is repeatedly associated with more apoptosis, not survival (PMID: 25066695, PMID: 24190542, PMID: 28867607). The contradictory study (PMID: 23723006) may be miscategorized or only partially contradictory, since it mainly challenges the 'decreased miR-1 favors survival' portion while actually showing decreased miR-133 in diseased hearts as well. Evidence item PMID: 28867607 and PMID: 35179668 have truncated abstracts, so conclusions from them should be treated more cautiously.

claim: Nitric oxide is an important toxic defense molecule against infectious organisms because activated macrophages release nitric oxide to inhibit pathogen replication.
support_summary: Multiple reviews and experimental/contextual studies describe nitric oxide (NO) as a key antimicrobial effector in innate immunity, stating that activated macrophages produce NO or reactive nitrogen intermediates that inhibit pathogen growth/replication or kill microbes; this is stated very directly in reviews on NO and immunity (PMID: 18341206, PMID: 11515807) and in macrophage resistance reviews noting mouse macrophage antimicrobial activity via inducible NO synthase/toxic nitrogen oxides (PMID: 8251575). Disease-focused reviews also support this theme, including tuberculosis, HIV-1, viral infection, and fish phagocyte literature, which describe macrophage/phagocyte-derived reactive nitrogen intermediates or NO as microbiostatic/microbicidal against bacteria, protozoa, fungi, parasites, and some viruses (PMID: 15520479, PMID: 8951805, PMID: 26208702, PMID: 11602197). Additional evidence is indirect but consistent: suppression of macrophage NO production by tick salivary extract is linked to impaired innate resistance and pathogen transmission (PMID: 9618727), and studies/reviews on Trichinella infection and phagocyte oxidative/nitrosative burst emphasize NO/free radicals as important for host defense (PMID: 17432248, PMID: 21972015). Overall, the recurring theme is that activated macrophages/phagocytes generate NO as a toxic antimicrobial mediator, though some abstracts frame this more generally as cytotoxic or microbiostatic activity rather than explicitly as inhibition of pathogen replication (PMID: 16822466, PMID: 15691589, PMID: 9701050).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
notes: Several support items are broad reviews of NO in inflammation/immunity and do not explicitly mention pathogen replication inhibition by macrophage-derived NO in the abstract, so they are better viewed as partial rather than direct support (e.g., PMID: 16822466, PMID: 15691589, PMID: 9701050). Some items have missing/empty abstracts, limiting confidence from abstract-only evidence (PMID: 8607373, PMID: 7553846). One important nuance is species specificity: the macrophage NO antimicrobial pathway is described clearly for mouse macrophages, while one review notes it had not yet been described in human macrophages at that time (PMID: 8251575), which tempers generalization rather than directly contradicting the claim.

claim: Nitric oxide regulates the functional activity, growth, and death of multiple immune and inflammatory cell types, including macrophages, T lymphocytes, antigen-presenting cells, mast cells, neutrophils, and natural killer cells.
support_summary: Multiple reviews directly state that nitric oxide (NO) regulates the functional activity, growth, and death of numerous immune and inflammatory cell types, explicitly listing macrophages, T lymphocytes, antigen-presenting cells, mast cells, neutrophils, and natural killer cells (PMID: 18341206; PMID: 11515807). Additional supporting reviews describe NO as broadly involved in immunity and inflammation, including macrophage antimicrobial activity, T-cell differentiation/activation/polarization, modulation of inflammatory mediator release, and effects on cell survival/apoptosis in immune cells (PMID: 15691589; PMID: 26208702; PMID: 29519621; PMID: 15962091; PMID: 8951805). Recurring themes are that NO is produced by activated immune cells, acts as both an effector and signaling molecule, and can influence immune-cell function and fate in a context- and concentration-dependent manner (PMID: 16822466; PMID: 10756925; PMID: 21972015). Overall, the abstract-level evidence strongly supports a broad immunoregulatory role for NO across multiple innate and adaptive immune cell types.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
notes: The strongest support comes from broad review articles whose abstracts explicitly mirror the claim wording (PMID: 18341206; PMID: 11515807). Many other cited items provide only partial or indirect abstract-level support because they focus on subsets of cells or functions—such as macrophages, phagocytes, T cells, or inflammatory mediator release—rather than all listed cell types and all outcomes of activity, growth, and death (e.g., PMID: 15691589; PMID: 29519621; PMID: 15962091). Some SUPPORT items have minimal or no abstract text available (PMID: 8607373; PMID: 7553846), so they cannot be weighed strongly from abstracts alone. No obvious miscategorized contradiction was identifiable from the provided abstracts.

claim: In prostate cancer, higher CXCR7/RDC1 expression is associated with more aggressive tumor behavior and with prostate cancer progression and metastasis.
support_summary: Multiple prostate cancer studies support a pro-tumor role for CXCR7, showing that CXCR7 promotes proliferation, motility, invasion, EMT, tumor growth, and metastatic behavior. In prostate cancer models, CXCR7 was required for androgen/CXCL12-driven motility and was proposed as a determinant of metastatic potential (PMID: 25884570), while obesity- and CXCL12-linked models showed high CXCR7 in tumors and demonstrated that CXCR7 knockdown or antagonism reduced invasion, EMT, and tumor growth (PMID: 28687617; PMID: 36088505). Additional prostate-specific evidence showed IL-8-induced CXCR7 promotes EGFR/ERK signaling and tumor growth, with CXCR7 depletion slowing xenograft growth (PMID: 21398406), and CRPC data reported CXCR7 overexpression in patient specimens and implicated the MIF/CXCR7/AKT pathway in growth and metastasis after ADT (PMID: 30224544). More indirect support comes from studies/reviews linking CXCR7 to transendothelial migration, tissue invasion, EMT, and metastasis-related processes, though not always specifically tied to expression-aggressiveness correlations in human prostate tumors (PMID: 19641136; PMID: 34920230; PMID: 18533280).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
notes: Most supporting articles are mechanistic or preclinical and support a role for CXCR7 in aggressive behavior rather than directly proving that higher CXCR7/RDC1 expression in human prostate tumors correlates with clinical progression/metastasis. PMID: 18533280 is a general review and not prostate-cancer-specific, so it is only indirect evidence. PMID: 34920230 appears to be broad/background-style and its abstract gives limited direct data on expression-outcome associations. Overall, there is no explicit contradiction in the supplied set, but the strongest support is for functional involvement of CXCR7 in progression/metastatic phenotypes rather than a clean clinical biomarker association.

claim: In prostate cancer cell models, increased CXCR7/RDC1 activity promotes cell adhesion, invasion, and survival.
support_summary: Several studies support a pro-tumor role for CXCR7 in prostate cancer models, though often alongside CXCR4 rather than as an isolated driver. In obese HiMyc-derived prostate cancer cells, CXCL12 stimulated migration/invasion and activated STAT3, NFκB, and MAPK signaling, and these effects were reduced by CXCR7 knockdown, implicating CXCR7 in invasive behavior and survival-related signaling (PMID: 28687617). In prostate cancer/allograft models, CXCR7 antagonism inhibited tumor growth and epithelial-mesenchymal transition, supporting a role in progression/aggressiveness (PMID: 36088505), and in castration-resistant prostate cancer, CXCR7 was upregulated and linked to MIF/AKT-driven growth and metastasis, with CXCR7 blockade suppressing tumor growth (PMID: 30224544). Broader non-prostate evidence also indicates that CXCR7 expression can enhance cell survival and adhesion, though not migration, across tumor models (PMID: 16940167), while a review notes links to tumor growth and survival more generally (PMID: 18533280).
contradict_summary: One prostate cancer study directly argues against the claim’s direction for motility: in LNCaP cells, dihydrotestosterone and flagellin decreased CXCR7 protein yet increased CXCL12-directed migration, leading the authors to propose that CXCR7 functions as a decoy receptor that counteracts CXCL12/CXCR4-mediated migration rather than promoting it (PMID: 31865399). This contradicts the idea that increased CXCR7 activity uniformly promotes invasive behavior in prostate cancer cell models.
notes: The evidence is mixed and context-dependent. Most supporting studies address invasion, tumor growth, EMT, or survival/growth signaling, but only limited evidence directly covers all parts of the claim, especially adhesion and survival specifically in prostate cancer cell models. Some supporting items are indirect or non-prostate-specific (PMID: 16940167, PMID: 18533280). The contradictory study focuses on migration rather than adhesion, invasion, or survival, but it still raises a mechanistic discrepancy by suggesting CXCR7 can restrain CXCL12-driven motility in at least one prostate cancer context (PMID: 31865399).

claim: In prostate cancer, CXCR7/RDC1 influences metastatic and angiogenic behavior through downstream effects on adhesion-related molecules, proangiogenic factors, and AKT signaling.
support_summary: Evidence directly in prostate cancer is limited but generally aligns with the claim that CXCR7 contributes to aggressive behavior. In prostate cancer, CXCR7/CXCL12 signaling was reported to participate in EMT and initiation of metastasis formation, linking the receptor to metastatic potential, though not specifically to angiogenic mediators or adhesion molecules in the abstract (PMID: 34920230). In castration-resistant prostate cancer, CXCR7 was described as an AR-repressed gene upregulated after ADT, with MIF-CXCR7 signaling activating AKT and promoting growth/metastasis, and CXCR7 blockade reduced tumor growth with possible anti-metastatic effects (PMID: 30224544). A broader review also notes CXCR7 may mediate tumor growth/survival signaling, but this is indirect and not prostate-cancer-specific (PMID: 18533280).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
notes: The strongest abstract-level support is for CXCR7 involvement in metastasis-related behavior and AKT signaling in prostate cancer, especially CRPC (PMID: 30224544). However, the provided abstracts do not directly demonstrate the specific downstream regulation of adhesion-related molecules or proangiogenic factors central to the claim; support for those components appears indirect or absent in these items. PMID: 18533280 is a general CXCR7 review rather than prostate-cancer-specific evidence, so it should be treated as only contextual support.

claim: Hypoxia induces mitochondrial autophagy in cells through hypoxia-inducible factor-1-dependent expression of BNIP3, together with constitutive expression of Beclin-1 and Atg5.
support_summary: Multiple studies support that hypoxia can activate autophagy/mitophagy through HIF-1/HIF-1α-dependent induction of BNIP3, including reports that HIF-1-deficient cells fail to induce BNIP3 or mitochondrial autophagy and do not reduce mitochondrial mass under hypoxia (PMID: 18376135), and reviews summarizing BNIP3-triggered selective mitochondrial autophagy downstream of HIF-1 (PMID: 20732359, PMID: 20153717). Several experimental systems likewise found that hypoxia or hypoxia-mimetic/ischemic stress increased HIF-1α, BNIP3, autophagy markers, and autophagosome formation, with autophagy reduced by HIF-1α or BNIP3 inhibition/knockdown (PMID: 31590128, PMID: 21465467, PMID: 31072291, PMID: 26323347, PMID: 38199116, PMID: 36468826). Some papers also connect this pathway to core autophagy machinery, showing Beclin1 is required for survival under hypoxia or is upregulated with hypoxia-associated autophagy, and Atg5 function/expression is implicated in hypoxia-responsive autophagy models (PMID: 19273585, PMID: 30531436, PMID: 21465467, PMID: 31072291). Overall, the strongest recurring theme is HIF-1/HIF-1α→BNIP3 as a common mediator of hypoxia-induced autophagy/mitophagy, whereas direct abstract-level support for the specific claim of constitutive Beclin-1 and Atg5 expression is more limited.
contradict_summary: Several studies directly challenge the claim's mechanism by reporting that hypoxia-induced autophagy or mitophagy can occur independently of HIF-1 and BNIP3, instead involving AMPK signaling or other pathways (PMID: 18551130, PMID: 24140568, PMID: 27687210). Other studies report that hypoxia decreases Beclin-1 expression rather than supporting a Beclin-1-associated induction mechanism, including malignant intestinal epithelial cells and trophoblast/JEG-3 models (PMID: 19901551, PMID: 19050324). One study found that under hypoxia plus glucose restriction, autophagy was inhibited with reduced PI3K-III/Beclin1 complex formation and blocked ATG12-ATG5 conjugation (PMID: 23500541). Additional partial contradictions suggest the roles of Beclin-1, ATG5, and BNIP3 in hypoxia-linked autophagy may be context-dependent rather than universally required as stated (PMID: 25115400, PMID: 23216071, PMID: 27366871).
notes: The evidence is heterogeneous and highly context-dependent across cell types, duration of hypoxia, and accompanying metabolic stress. Several supporting items are reviews or discuss general autophagy rather than specifically mitochondrial autophagy, and many do not address the claim's precise wording about constitutive Beclin-1 and Atg5 expression. A notable discrepancy is PMID: 37150840, which is placed in the contradicting set but its abstract actually reports that HIF-1α silencing under hypoxia lowers BNIP3, Beclin1, LC3II/I, and autophagosomes, which is more supportive than contradictory. Overall, the HIF-1/BNIP3 axis is well supported in many systems, but universality of this mechanism and the exact Beclin-1/Atg5 component are not consistently confirmed.

claim: In cells exposed to prolonged hypoxia, mitochondrial autophagy functions as an adaptive metabolic response that helps prevent increased reactive oxygen species and cell death.
support_summary: Multiple abstracts support the claim that hypoxia-triggered mitophagy is an adaptive response that reduces mitochondrial load/respiration, limits ROS, and promotes survival. The strongest evidence is from HIF-1/BNIP3 studies showing that under chronic hypoxia, failure to induce BNIP3-dependent mitochondrial autophagy leads to excess ROS and cell death, whereas induction of mitophagy maintains redox homeostasis and viability (PMID: 18376135; PMID: 20732359). Additional studies in hepatocytes and other models report that increased mitochondrial autophagy during hypoxia or hypoxia/reoxygenation lowers mitochondrial respiration/ROS and protects against injury or apoptosis (PMID: 23589298; PMID: 25561470). Several other papers are more indirect but consistent, linking hypoxia-induced BNIP3/BNIP3L-, HIF-, or AMPK-dependent autophagy/mitophagy with survival under prolonged hypoxic stress (PMID: 19273585; PMID: 24140568; PMID: 23683277; PMID: 30109811).
contradict_summary: Some evidence indicates that hypoxia-induced autophagy can become deleterious or context-dependent rather than uniformly protective. In endothelial cells, autophagy was pro-survival early in hypoxia but, under prolonged hypoxia, correlated with increased cell death, and inhibiting autophagy reduced late death (PMID: 23408345). In cardiomyocytes, hypoxia-induced BNIP3/autophagy was linked to mitochondria-dependent apoptosis, and autophagy inhibition decreased apoptosis (PMID: 27366871). Other reports suggest hypoxia may suppress autophagy under severe metabolic stress or that BNIP3/BNIP3L can contribute to hypoxia-associated cell death in certain developmental, cancer, or ischemia/reperfusion contexts (PMID: 23500541; PMID: 25394489; PMID: 25684163; PMID: 19641497; PMID: 19901551).
notes: The evidence is heterogeneous across cell type and stress context: the most direct support for the specific claim comes from chronic hypoxia/HIF-1/BNIP3 studies in fibroblasts and redox-stress hepatocyte models, whereas several supporting items are broader hypoxia-autophagy papers or ischemia/reperfusion models rather than prolonged hypoxia alone. Some SUPPORT-group items discuss general autophagy rather than explicit mitochondrial autophagy or do not directly measure ROS/cell death, so they are indirect support. Some CONTRADICT-group items involve hypoxia/reoxygenation, glucose restriction plus hypoxia, developmental cavitation, or review-level discussion, which are not exact matches to the claim and may reflect different severity/duration of stress. Overall, discrepancies likely reflect context dependence: adaptive mitophagy appears protective in many chronic hypoxia settings, but excessive or differently regulated autophagy can contribute to death in other models.

claim: In adult outpatients with hypertension, a structured self-reported medication adherence measure was associated with blood pressure control, supporting concurrent and predictive validity for identifying poor blood pressure control.
support_summary: Multiple studies in adults with hypertension reported that structured self-reported adherence scales were associated with better blood pressure control, including the original 4-item Morisky measure showing both concurrent and predictive validity over years of follow-up (PMID: 3945130) and a 24-month longitudinal study where baseline nonadherence predicted higher systolic BP over time (PMID: 22107599). Cross-sectional validation studies of MMAS-8 translations in Brazil, Korea, Iran, and rural Korea consistently found significant associations between lower adherence scores and poorer BP control, with some reporting useful sensitivity/specificity or strong adjusted odds ratios for poor control (PMID: 24268603; PMID: 24463958; PMID: 25946926; PMID: 24299439). Additional observational studies using self-report or related adherence instruments also found adherent patients had lower systolic/diastolic BP or better BP control in primary care/outpatient populations (PMID: 16553506; PMID: 19479901; PMID: 25585359; PMID: 26307159; PMID: 39838337). Overall, the main theme is reproducible concurrent association across settings and some evidence of predictive validity, especially for Morisky-type self-report tools.
contradict_summary: A qualitative Swedish study found no obvious relation between reported adherence, laboratory markers of adherence, and blood pressure levels, despite exploring reasons for adherence/nonadherence in hypertensive patients (PMID: 11104870). A cross-sectional study in rural Rwanda reported high MMAS-8 self-reported adherence but poor overall BP control, and no patient factor was statistically associated with poor BP control, suggesting no detectable adherence-BP association in that setting (PMID: 31638907). The main contradictory theme is that self-reported adherence may not track BP control well in some small or resource-limited populations where other factors may dominate.
notes: The evidence base is largely supportive, with the strongest and most directly relevant abstract being the original validation study itself (PMID: 3945130). Several SUPPORT items are only indirectly relevant because they assess interventions, refill adherence, self-efficacy, or general adherence-BP relationships rather than directly validating a structured self-reported adherence measure against BP control (e.g., PMID: 21365405, PMID: 20823391, PMID: 16623608, PMID: 16623608, PMID: 23696676). One SUPPORT item appears mischaracterized: the Hong Kong study used MMAS-8 but the abstract does not clearly state that MMAS-8 adherence was independently associated with BP control (PMID: 25585359). The contradicting studies are fewer and may reflect methodological/contextual issues such as qualitative design, small samples, and strong health-system barriers.

claim: In adult outpatients with hypertension, the medication adherence measure showed good reliability.
support_summary: Several validation studies in hypertensive adult outpatient/primary care populations reported acceptable to strong reliability for medication adherence measures, including Hill-Bone, MMAS-8, MASES, MAR-Scale, and TASHP. Reported internal consistency was often in the acceptable-to-good range, such as Cronbach's alpha 0.72 for the Hill-Bone medication adherence subscale in Turkish primary care patients (PMID: 20055720), 0.92 for the Turkish MASES in outpatients (PMID: 19046930), >0.7 for the Malaysian MAR-Scale (PMID: 25902719), and >0.7 for TASHP with satisfactory split-half reliability in Chinese patients with hypertension (PMID: 26307159).
