claim: In patients with metastatic breast cancer receiving first-line chemotherapy, high-dose cyclophosphamide, mitoxantrone, and etoposide with hematopoietic rescue produced longer response duration than conventional-dose cyclophosphamide, mitoxantrone, and vincristine.
support_summary: A review of randomized high-dose chemotherapy in breast cancer reports that, in the trial comparing initial high-dose cyclophosphamide, mitoxantrone, and etoposide with conventional-dose cyclophosphamide, mitoxantrone, and vincristine, the high-dose approach was more effective in delaying time to progression and was the only setting among the two reviewed trials to show a survival effect (PMID: 9272140). This supports the broader idea that first-line high-dose therapy improved disease control relative to the conventional regimen, although the abstract does not explicitly state response duration.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
notes: The only cited article is a review rather than the primary trial, and its abstract discusses delayed time to progression and survival rather than explicitly reporting longer response duration. Thus, it is indirect/partial support for the claim rather than a direct confirmation of the specific endpoint.

claim: In patients with metastatic breast cancer receiving first-line chemotherapy, high-dose cyclophosphamide, mitoxantrone, and etoposide with hematopoietic rescue produced longer overall survival than conventional-dose cyclophosphamide, mitoxantrone, and vincristine.
support_summary: One review states that in a recently published randomized study, first-line high-dose cyclophosphamide, mitoxantrone, and etoposide with hematopoietic rescue was more effective than conventional-dose cyclophosphamide, mitoxantrone, and vincristine in delaying progression, and that this initial-treatment trial also showed an effect on survival (PMID: 9272140). This provides indirect support for the claim, although the abstract summarizes rather than details the original trial results.
contradict_summary: Later randomized evidence and overviews generally do not confirm an overall survival advantage for high-dose chemotherapy with hematopoietic support in metastatic breast cancer. A randomized trial found no significant survival or time-to-progression benefit for high-dose chemotherapy plus autologous stem-cell rescue versus conventional-dose maintenance chemotherapy after induction response (PMID: 10760307). An individual-patient overview of six randomized trials reported no statistically significant overall survival improvement despite better progression-free survival (PMID: 21768454), and a review likewise concluded there was no definitive evidence of survival advantage for high-dose chemotherapy with stem-cell support in metastatic breast cancer (PMID: 12182684).
notes: The supporting item (PMID: 9272140) is a review discussing a prior randomized study rather than the primary trial itself, so its support is indirect. The contradicting studies use different high-dose and control regimens, and some involve post-induction randomization rather than the exact first-line cyclophosphamide/mitoxantrone/etoposide versus cyclophosphamide/mitoxantrone/vincristine comparison in the claim, so they mainly challenge the broader proposition that high-dose chemotherapy with hematopoietic rescue improves overall survival in metastatic breast cancer.

claim: Children with previously normal development who developed regressive developmental disorder had associated gastrointestinal disease characterized by intestinal inflammation and lymphoid nodular hyperplasia.
support_summary: One small study discussed children described as having "autistic enterocolitis," defined in the abstract as autism with developmental regression and gastrointestinal symptoms, and reported measles RNA in peripheral blood mononuclear cells in 3 of 9 such children (PMID: 10759242). However, the abstract does not itself document intestinal inflammation or lymphoid nodular hyperplasia in these children, so it only indirectly aligns with the claim.
contradict_summary: A population-based study specifically tested whether regressive autism was associated with gastrointestinal symptoms or inflammatory bowel disease and found no such pattern (PMID: 11581466). Gastrointestinal symptoms occurred in 18.8% of children, constipation was most common, no inflammatory bowel disorder was reported, and regression was not associated with gastrointestinal symptoms; the authors concluded there was no evidence for a distinct "autistic enterocolitis" syndrome (PMID: 11581466).
notes: The supporting item appears only partially relevant because its abstract references "autistic enterocolitis" but does not report the claim's key pathology findings of intestinal inflammation and lymphoid nodular hyperplasia. The contradicting study addresses a broader syndrome question epidemiologically rather than directly performing intestinal pathology, but its findings are still in clear tension with the claim.

claim: The children’s behavioral regression, including autism or related developmental disorders, was temporally associated in many cases with possible environmental triggers such as measles, mumps, and rubella vaccination or measles infection.
support_summary: Direct supporting evidence from abstracts is limited. One laboratory study reported that some children with autism and enterocolitis had developmental regression and gastrointestinal symptoms, in some cases soon after MMR vaccination, and detected measles-virus sequences in 3 of 9 autistic children, with sequences consistent with vaccine strains and exposure history (PMID: 10759242). Another review of vaccine injury claims found a statistically significant clustering of acute neurologic symptom onset on days 8–9 after measles-containing vaccination, with some survivors showing mental regression, but this concerned encephalopathy rather than autism specifically (PMID: 9481001).
contradict_summary: Several epidemiologic and review articles argue against a temporal or causal association between MMR vaccination and autism/regressive developmental disorders. A large North Thames study of 498 cases found no step-up in autism after MMR introduction, no difference in age at diagnosis by vaccination status, no temporal clustering of autism onset within 1–2 years after MMR, and no clustering of developmental regression in the months after vaccination (PMID: 10376617). Another study found no evidence for a distinct regressive, gastrointestinal 'MMR-induced autism' phenotype: regression rates, age of first parental concern, interval from immunization to symptoms, and gastrointestinal findings were not associated with MMR exposure (PMID: 11581466). Additional reviews/editorials also state there is no epidemiologic or clinical evidence linking MMR to autism (PMIDs: 9624080, 10681219, 9643797, 10743285, 10373156, 9592960, 10376608).
notes: Most support is indirect, sparse, or based on non-abstract information; only PMID: 10759242 directly mentions regression occurring in some cases soon after MMR. PMID: 9481001 addresses rare post-vaccine encephalopathy with mental regression, which is not the same as autism or developmental disorders in the claim. Several cited contradicting items lack abstracts and appear to be reviews/editorials rather than primary epidemiologic studies, so the strongest contradiction comes from PMIDs: 10376617 and 11581466. The claim also mentions measles infection, but the provided evidence set is focused mainly on MMR vaccination rather than natural measles infection.

claim: Children with regressive developmental disorder and gastrointestinal disease generally had no focal neurological abnormalities and normal magnetic resonance imaging and electroencephalography findings.
support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
contradict_summary: The only cited contradicting article is a case report of acute disseminated encephalomyelitis after probable measles vaccine failure, describing marked focal neurological abnormalities and abnormal brain/spinal MRI findings in a 10-year-old boy (PMID: 10371390). However, this abstract does not concern children with regressive developmental disorder and gastrointestinal disease, so it does not directly address the claim population or context.
notes: There is a major relevance discrepancy: PMID: 10371390 appears miscategorizied as contradiction because it describes a different disease entity (ADEM after measles infection/vaccine failure) rather than the specific population in the claim. No evidence about EEG findings in the claim population was provided.

claim: Mouse embryonic stem cells deficient in BRCA1 have defective transcription-coupled repair of oxidative DNA damage compared with BRCA1-proficient cells.
support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
notes: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

claim: Mouse embryonic stem cells deficient in BRCA1 are more sensitive to ionizing radiation and hydrogen peroxide than BRCA1-proficient cells.
support_summary: Multiple review and mechanistic papers describe BRCA1 as a key regulator of DNA double-strand break repair and genome stability, and they report that BRCA1-defective cells show hypersensitivity to DNA-damaging agents, especially radiation, which is consistent with the claim but largely indirect rather than specific to mouse embryonic stem cells or hydrogen peroxide (PMID: 16254187, PMID: 16103751). One review explicitly states that human cancer cells and mouse cells deficient in BRCA1 exhibit radiation hypersensitivity (PMID: 11156530). A mechanistic study further supports radiation sensitivity by showing that a phosphorylation-site mutant BRCA1 failed to rescue the radiation hypersensitivity of a BRCA1-deficient cell line after ionizing radiation (PMID: 10550055).
contradict_summary: One study in embryonic stem cells found that loss of BRCA1 ubiquitin E3 ligase activity did not abolish viability or homologous recombination after ionizing radiation, and these cells were not hypersensitive to mitomycin C (PMID: 19088202). However, this paper tested cells expressing BRCA1 protein lacking enzymatic activity rather than fully BRCA1-deficient cells, and the abstract does not directly report resistance or sensitivity to hydrogen peroxide (PMID: 19088202).
notes: The supporting set is mostly indirect evidence from reviews rather than direct tests of the exact claim in mouse embryonic stem cells exposed to ionizing radiation and hydrogen peroxide. The contradicting paper appears mismatched to the claim because it concerns BRCA1 E3 ligase-dead cells, not complete BRCA1 deficiency; its abstract also does not clearly state lack of ionizing-radiation hypersensitivity, so the Med-V1 contradiction may be overstated (PMID: 19088202). No provided abstract directly addresses hydrogen peroxide sensitivity in mouse embryonic stem cells.

claim: BRCA1 participates directly or indirectly in transcription-coupled repair of oxidative DNA damage in cells.
support_summary: Direct support comes from studies showing that BRCA1-deficient human cells are impaired in transcription-coupled repair (TCR) of oxidative lesions, especially 8-oxoguanine on the transcribed strand, with restoration by wild-type BRCA1 expression (PMID: 11034101). A related study found BRCA1-mutant cells are radiosensitive and specifically defective in TCR rather than double-strand break repair, linking BRCA1-dependent TCR to resistance to oxidative damage from ionizing radiation (PMID: 10373498). Additional evidence shows BRCA1-mutant lymphoblasts accumulate oxidatively induced lesions including 8-hydroxy-2'-deoxyguanosine and cyclopurine lesions after irradiation, supporting a role in repair of oxidative DNA damage and possibly nucleotide excision-related processing of such lesions (PMID: 17288454). Broader mechanistic and review papers support indirect participation by placing BRCA1 in TCR-associated pathways, interactions with RNA polymerase II/repair complexes, and transcriptional regulation of oxidative DNA repair genes such as BER components and NER genes (PMID: 20185827, PMID: 10490643, PMID: 10783165, PMID: 12496474).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
notes: The strongest abstract-level evidence for the specific claim is PMID: 11034101, with PMID: 10373498 also strongly supportive but focused on ionizing-radiation-associated TCR rather than naming a specific oxidative base lesion. Many other cited items are reviews or discuss BRCA1 in general DNA repair, UV-induced TCR, BER regulation, or transcription-associated damage without specifically addressing transcription-coupled repair of oxidative DNA damage; these are better treated as indirect/partial support rather than direct confirmation. No clear abstract-level discrepancies contradict the claim, but some SUPPORT items appear only tangentially relevant to oxidative TCR.

claim: In Nicotiana benthamiana plants, potato virus Y infection suppresses post-transcriptional gene silencing of a green fluorescent protein transgene through the HCPro viral protein.
support_summary: Several abstracts support the general mechanism that potyviral HC-Pro suppresses post-transcriptional gene silencing (PTGS) in plants, including direct statements that HC-Pro is a PTGS suppressor (PMID: 11021800) and that potyvirus P1/HC-Pro suppresses PTGS at the posttranscriptional level in transgenic plants (PMID: 9827799). In Nicotiana benthamiana, PVY HC-Pro is reported to suppress antiviral silencing, with suppression correlating with its RNA-binding properties, although the provided abstract is truncated and does not explicitly mention a GFP transgene (PMID: 28381573). Comparative work in N. benthamiana also shows that PVY HC-Pro enhances GFP and GUS expression similarly to other silencing suppressors, consistent with suppression of RNA silencing affecting transgene expression (PMID: 22354861). Overall, the evidence supports HC-Pro as the mediator of silencing suppression, but most abstracts are indirect for the specific claim about PVY infection suppressing PTGS of a GFP transgene in N. benthamiana.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
notes: Most supporting items are indirect or mechanistic rather than direct replications of the exact claim; several discuss HC-Pro or potyviruses broadly rather than PVY infection of a GFP transgene in Nicotiana benthamiana. PMID: 28381573 has a truncated abstract, limiting confidence about details. PMID: 29382787 is a correction notice rather than primary evidence. Some items appear somewhat miscategorized as support for the exact claim because they concern other viruses, other transgenes, or general reviews rather than the specific PVY/GFP/N. benthamiana experiment.

claim: In Nicotiana benthamiana plants, cucumber mosaic virus infection suppresses post-transcriptional gene silencing of a green fluorescent protein transgene through the 2b viral protein.
support_summary: Multiple studies support that cucumber mosaic virus (CMV) 2b suppresses RNA/post-transcriptional gene silencing in Nicotiana benthamiana, including transgene silencing, and that this activity is important for viral spread and virulence. The strongest direct evidence is that CMV 2b was previously reported to suppress PTGS initiation in transgenic N. benthamiana and that its nuclear localization is required for efficient suppression (PMID: 10747034); another study states explicitly that Cmv2b is a nuclear protein that suppresses transgene RNA silencing in N. benthamiana (PMID: 11386367). Additional work shows CMV or CMV 2b can enhance GFP/GUS expression, eliminate or reduce silencing-associated siRNAs, prevent systemic silencing, and reactivate silenced transgenes, all consistent with suppression of PTGS by 2b (PMID: 22354861, PMID: 11823432, PMID: 13679623, PMID: 19059248).
contradict_summary: There is no strong abstract-level evidence directly contradicting the claim in N. benthamiana. One study concerns Drosophila cells and reports CMV 2b does not suppress RNAi there, but this is in a different organism and assay and does not refute plant PTGS suppression (PMID: 15331016). Another CMV/TuMV coinfection study actually notes that functional 2b behaves as expected for an RNA silencing suppressor, while also proposing an additional 2b role in vascular unloading independent of RNA silencing, so it does not clearly contradict the claim (PMID: 21916556).
notes: The cited retraction (PMID: 26286615) has no abstract details here and is too nonspecific to use as direct contradiction of this exact claim; it should be treated cautiously. Several supporting studies do not specifically mention a GFP transgene in the abstract, but they consistently support the broader mechanism that CMV 2b suppresses transgene/PTGS in N. benthamiana. There is also a categorization discrepancy: PMID: 21916556 appears misfiled under contradiction because its abstract includes supportive statements about 2b as an RNA silencing suppressor.

claim: The viral suppressors act at different stages of post-transcriptional gene silencing in Nicotiana benthamiana plants, with HCPro blocking maintenance of established silencing and 2b preventing initiation of silencing in growing tissues.
support_summary: Abstract evidence supports the 2b portion of the claim: CMV 2b is described as a suppressor of PTGS initiation in transgenic Nicotiana benthamiana, and its nuclear localization is required for efficient suppression (PMID: 10747034). Additional studies report that CMV/PSV 2b suppresses local RNA silencing, eliminates associated siRNAs, prevents systemic silencing, and blocks the activity or spread of the mobile silencing signal, consistent with interference early in silencing establishment and spread (PMID: 22354861, PMID: 11823432). Across these abstracts, a recurring theme is that 2b acts on initiation/signaling phases of silencing rather than merely downstream effects.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
notes: The provided support abstracts do not directly demonstrate the HCPro-specific part of the claim, namely that HCPro blocks maintenance of established silencing in Nicotiana benthamiana. PMID: 22354861 includes HC-Pro in a comparative suppressor assay, but its abstract mainly shows strong suppression similar to other suppressors and does not explicitly establish the maintenance-stage mechanism. No clear evidence discrepancy was found within the listed abstracts, but the overall support is partial because the stage-specific contrast between HCPro and 2b is not fully shown from these abstracts alone.
